businessModel.text:
Ford operates across three primary vehicle segments following its organisational restructuring:
  
    Ford Blue (ICE): Traditional internal combustion engine vehicles — F-Series trucks, Mustang, Explorer, Bronco — the cash-generating backbone of the company. Ford Blue generates the profits and free cash flow that funds the EV transition. The segment is in structural long-term decline as consumer preferences shift.
    Ford Model e (EVs): Electric vehicles including the F-150 Lightning (electric truck), Mustang Mach-E, and E-Transit van. This segment is currently loss-making and is expected to remain so until EV scale reaches the level required to achieve manufacturing cost parity with ICE vehicles.
    Ford Pro (Commercial): A dedicated commercial vehicle and fleet services business selling ICE and EV commercial vehicles globally, alongside telematics and service contracts. This is a high-margin, sticky business that benefits from the transition to electric commercial fleets.
    Ford Credit: The captive finance arm provides auto financing and leasing to Ford customers and dealers. It provides a steady earnings contribution and helps support vehicle sales through promotional leasing programmes.
  
  Ford's core strategic challenge is managing the simultaneous decline of its ICE cash cow and the losses of its EV growth engine — a transition that has proven far more difficult and expensive than originally modelled.

thesisEvaluation.text:
Bull Case
      60 / 100
      Ford Model e achieves breakeven by 2027 as EV manufacturing scale drives costs down. The F-Series remains the best-selling truck in the US, generating strong cash flow to fund the transition. Ford Pro commercial business grows at 10–15 per cent annually. The stock re-rates modestly as the market gains confidence in the EV timeline, driving the shares to $14–16.
      Weight: 30%
    
    
      Base Case
      38 / 100
      EV losses remain elevated at $3–5bn annually through 2027. ICE truck and SUV margins compress further as Chinese EVs enter the US market with affordable options. Ford Blue cash flow is insufficient to fund Ford Model e losses and capital expenditure simultaneously. The dividend is cut or suspended. Shares drift to $9–11 as earnings disappointment persists.
      Weight: 50%
    
    
      Bear Case
      25 / 100
      EV adoption slows as charging infrastructure gaps and range anxiety limit consumer uptake. Ford Model e losses become existential, exceeding $6bn annually. Chinese EVs capture 15–20 per cent of the US market with low-priced vehicles Ford cannot compete with. Ford Blue cash flow collapses. The company requires emergency capital raise or strategic partnership at distressed terms.
      Weight: 20%

recommendation.text:
Conviction Score: 40/100 — SELL
  Ford trades 18 per cent below its 52-week high with negative earnings, expanding EV losses, and Chinese OEM competition intensifying. The ICE cash cow that funds the transition is under pressure from all sides. The risk-reward does not support a buy, and existing holders should use any rallies as opportunities to reduce exposure. The SELL conviction is driven by the combination of near-term losses and structural competitive headwinds.
  
    Buy Below$9
    HOLD Zone$9–$12
    Reduce Above$12
    Stop / Sell$8

entryExit.text:
F — Ford Motor Company (F) — Entry / Exit Framework

Reduce / Trim: Above $12.37 — the stock is trading near or above the 52-week high. Capital should be rotated elsewhere.
Tighten or exit on any bounce above $13.34 — the risk/reward is poor at current levels.
Stop-loss (for remaining exposure): $15.1 — a close above the 52-week high would confirm the bull case is breaking down.
Target: $9.94 — partial mean reversion toward the middle of the 52-week range.
